text: The surgeon creates a small stab wound over the inferior crease of the umbilicus and inserts a small-sized Veress needle. After hearing two click sounds, irrigation suction and the hanging drop test are performed, followed by the initiation of pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon introduces the trocar into the abdomen, revealing a retrocecal and pelvic appendix. The appendix tip appears inflamed, and free fluid is visible in the surrounding area.

The surgeon holds the kinked, elongated appendix in the middle, using a harmonic scalpel with the vibrating jaw oriented towards the iliac vessel for dissection. The ilium is visible below, and the tinea coli can be seen on the cecum.
The surgical field shows the appendix with its base not yet reached during dissection. In pediatric patients, the bladder is typically intra-abdominal, which may be visible in the surgical view.

The surgeon carefully inserts the trocar into the left iliac fossa, revealing the base of the appendix. Using two ports and a Striker mini alligator positioned in the right hypochondrium for traction, the major appendix is dissected up to its base to prevent stump appendicitis.

The surgeon uses a Melgers knot to secure the base of the long, inflamed appendix, ensuring no stump is left. A striker mini alligator is introduced into the loop to assist in tightening the knot at the appendix base. The knot is tightened multiple times and then cut with scissors. The harmonic device is used to dissect the appendix, burning the mucosa to prevent mucosal remnants. The appendix is separated, leaving a stump of only three to five millimeters.

The surgeon changes the telescope to a 5mm scope while a striker mini alligator lifts the appendix. Using a claw forceps through the 10mm umbilical port, the appendix is carefully drawn into the cannula, ensuring no transverse arrest occurs. The cannula is then removed from the abdominal cavity.
